content.heading: Data Types — what kind of thing is this?

body.text: Every value in JavaScript has a type. The type determines what you can do with it. You can't do math with text, and you can't use string methods on a number. Knowing types helps you avoid bugs that seem totally random but have a very simple cause.

rows: True or false — only two options
A variable was created but never given a value

body.desc: The typeof operator tells you what type a value is. It returns a string like 'number', 'string', or 'boolean'.

lines: let a = '5';   // this is a string
let b = 5;     // this is a number

body.label: The most common beginner trap
body.text: If a number comes from user input or an HTML field, it arrives as a STRING. So '5' + 5 gives you '55'. Always check your types when math isn't working the way you expect.

exercises.title: Identify the types
tasks: Before running: write down the type you expect for each variable.
Then run and use typeof to check each one.
exercises.hint: Remember: anything in quotes is a string, even if it looks like a number.

exercises.title: Fix the broken math
tasks: The code below should add two numbers together and get 15.
Run it first and see what you get.
Use typeof to figure out what's wrong.
Fix it so the result is actually 15.
exercises.hint: The Number() function converts a string to a number: Number('10') gives you 10.

exercises.title: Booleans
tasks: Declare a variable called gameOver and set it to false.
Declare a variable called playerAlive and set it to true.
Log both variables and their types.
Now try: console.log('false' === false). What do you get? Why?
Flip gameOver to true and log it again.
exercises.hint: true and false (without quotes) are booleans. 'true' and 'false' (with quotes) are strings — completely different things.

exercises.title: Type detective
tasks: Create one variable of each type: string, number, boolean, and leave one undefined.
Use typeof on all four and log the results with labels.
Now try typeof on some expressions: typeof (5 + 3), typeof ('hi' + 5), typeof (5 > 3).
Predict the result before running each one.
exercises.hint: An expression like (5 > 3) evaluates to true or false — so its type is boolean.

challenges.title: Why does 'true' behave differently than true?
challenges.description: The code below checks if something is true, but it never works. Figure out why.
challenges.hint: What type is 'true' in quotes? What type does the condition check for?
challenges.explanation: 'true' (in quotes) is a string. true (without quotes) is a boolean. They are completely different values. === checks type AND value, so 'true' === true is false. Fix: remove the quotes from 'true' on line 1 so isReady is a real boolean.

challenges.title: The total should be 100 but it's '5050'
challenges.description: Someone tried to add two scores but got a weird result. Diagnose and fix it using typeof.
challenges.hint: Use typeof to check what score1 and score2 actually are. Then use Number() to fix them.
challenges.explanation: Both score1 and score2 are strings because they're in quotes. String + string = concatenation. '50' + '50' = '5050'. Fix: either remove the quotes (let score1 = 50) or convert with Number(): total = Number(score1) + Number(score2).
